payload.description:
# Plexus as Runtime — Single-tool MCP via Tactus code execution

## Thesis

Replace the current ~64-tool Plexus MCP server surface with a single `execute_tactus` MCP tool that exposes Plexus functionality as a programmable Tactus Lua API.

> Plexus becomes a runtime platform for agentic code execution rather than a machine with a bunch of tools sticking out of it.

## Why

The current 64-tool MCP catalog burns context budget before any real work begins (industry reports show 50k–150k tokens of tool definitions are common in tool-rich MCP setups; Anthropic measured 98.7% reductions on real workflows by switching to code execution). More importantly, a flat tool catalog cannot express:

- Sub-agent composition (Tactus is fundamentally an agent-orchestration DSL — each Worker is its own LLM with its own context window, tools, and model choice).
- The full Plexus ML ladder (regex → classical ML → embeddings → fine-tuned BERT → small LLM → big LLM → multi-agent) as cost-aware first-class primitives.
- Train-evaluate-deploy loops as single coherent programs.
- Versioned, savable, reusable agent workflows that close the loop back into Procedures.

## Scope (everything is v1, no v2-deferred bucket)

All ~56 substantive Plexus operations are exposed in the v1 Lua API surface, including the dispatched/long-running ones (evaluations, reports, procedures). HITL-by-default for ~14 destructive operations. Distributed budget infrastructure is mandatory v1, not deferred — running evaluations is core to Plexus.

## Architectural commitments

1. **One MCP tool** (`execute_tactus`) with a small boot prompt (≤5k tokens hard ceiling) and on-demand docs via `plexus.docs.*`.
2. **Tactus host-module mechanism (Tier 1)** — minimal upstream Tactus change to allow `local plexus = require('plexus')` from sandboxed Lua.
3. **PlexusModule facade** — ~14 namespaces (`plexus.scorecards.*`, `plexus.score.*`, `plexus.evaluation.*`, `plexus.feedback.*`, `plexus.predict`, `plexus.dataset.*`, `plexus.docs.*`, `plexus.budget.*`, `plexus.cost.*`, etc.) — sharing service implementations with the existing MCP tools (no duplicate logic).
4. **Hierarchical, divisible, ambient budgets** with categories (usd, wallclock, tokens, llm_calls, tool_calls, depth, workers). Distributed propagation through the existing task-dispatch system.
5. **HITL-by-default for destructive operations** — auto-fired `Human.approve` before mutations; `no_confirm = true` escape hatch with audit logging.
6. **Streaming + handle ergonomics** for long-running operations (wire trace bridge to MCP streaming; handle-based async for very long ops).
7. **Dual-stack migration** — old 64 tools coexist; new tool is opt-in; sunset criteria written down.

## Key safety properties

- Conservative default budget (~`$0.25 / 60s / depth 3`) so a forgetful caller cannot accidentally burn money.
- Account-level caps independent of per-call budgets.
- Recursion depth tracked across dispatch boundaries.
- Tracing recorded for every `execute_tactus` call (source, calls, costs, errors) for replay/debug.
- Module bindings carry per-invocation context immutably (no cross-tenant leak via captured references).

## Out of scope (separate initiatives, noted as comments below)

- Tactus package system Tier 2 (full third-party package system with manifests, versioning, distribution, capabilities)
- Existing MCP server consolidation (drift between `MCP/server.py` / `MCP/asgi_app.py` / `MCP/plexus_fastmcp_server.py`, dead code, dual `get_plexus_documentation`)
- Cost-aware promotion gates (cost-per-decision metric, volume projection at promotion, per-score daily ceilings, anomaly detection — the "scaling-aware design" track)

## Proceed criterion

Epic 1 (empirical validation spike) is gating. ≥85% first-try success rate from frontier LLMs writing real Plexus-flavored Tactus Lua against a stub PlexusModule + 2k-token boot prompt + on-demand docs is required to commit to the rest of the initiative.

## Rough scope estimate

5–7 weeks of focused work for the full v1, dominated by the distributed-budget infrastructure (Epic 4). The Tactus host-module work (Epic 2) is small (~50 lines + tests, ~1–2 days). Service-layer refactor (within Epic 3) for ~15 of the existing tools is the second-largest cost.
payload.title: Plexus as Runtime: Single-tool MCP via Tactus code execution
